title: US closes chip loophole, EU declares tech sovereignty, undersea cables face new threats
subhead: Washington tightened AI chip export controls to cover Chinese subsidiaries in Malaysia and Southeast Asia, while the European Commission unveiled a tech sovereignty package and Iran's threats to undersea cables in the Strait of Hormuz pushed Big Tech to seek alternative routes.

sections.heading: US closes Blackwell chip loophole via Malaysia
sections.body:
The US Commerce Department announced new guidelines on May 31 extending advanced AI chip export controls to overseas subsidiaries of companies headquartered in China, closing a regulatory gap that had allowed Chinese firms to secure NVIDIA's Blackwell chips through subsidiaries in Malaysia, Singapore, and the Middle East. The 'AI proliferation' rules introduced late in the Biden administration were effectively not enforced during the Trump administration's first year, according to Asia Business Daily.

Industry sources suggest hundreds of thousands of AI chips may have flowed to China-related companies over the past year through this conduit. Chris McGuire, a former State Department official, called the loophole 'a huge problem' on X and urged verification of actual quantities shipped. The guidelines do not prohibit use of already-installed chips, avoiding immediate disruption to existing data center operations.

Separately, Senator Elizabeth Warren invited NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang to testify before the Senate Banking Committee on June 11 about the company's China sales and export control posture. Warren told CNBC she is concerned about chips 'used for military purposes' in China and that US companies profiting from such sales 'undermines our long-term security.'
sections.whyItMatters: The closure of this loophole tightens the enforcement architecture of US export controls, but raises questions about how many advanced chips have already reached Chinese-controlled entities and whether enforcement can keep pace with corporate restructuring.

sections.heading: EU unveils tech sovereignty package
sections.body:
The European Commission on June 3 proposed a European Technological Sovereignty Package aimed at reducing dependence on US and Chinese providers for chips, AI, and cloud computing. The package includes a Cloud and AI Development Act (CADA) and Chips Act 2.0, according to CNBC.

Executive Vice-President Henna Virkkunen told reporters the Commission wants to ensure cloud providers handling critical workloads don't have a 'kill switch.' She noted it would be difficult for US companies to reach the highest sovereignty levels due to the US Cloud Act, which allows American law enforcement to request data from US companies regardless of storage location. Commission President Ursula von der Leyen stated: 'We cannot afford to depend on others for the technologies that keep our hospitals running, our energy grids stable and our services secure.'

China's foreign ministry warned against 'protectionism' in response, with EU Observer reporting that Beijing sees the EU's tech sovereignty drive as squeezing foreign suppliers.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe is attempting to operationalize digital sovereignty through regulatory architecture, but the challenge of building indigenous alternatives to US cloud and chip infrastructure remains formidable. The reference to 'kill switches' reflects growing European concern about extraterritorial US legal reach.

sections.heading: Iran threatens undersea cable fees in Strait of Hormuz
sections.body:
Iran has announced intentions to impose license fees on undersea internet cables running through the Strait of Hormuz, specifically naming Meta, Google, Amazon, and Microsoft as targets, according to Ars Technica. Iranian state media outlets Tasnim and Fars proposed charging tech giants for cable usage while claiming Iran alone has the right to repair and maintain subsea cables in the area.

The threat has already had material effects: Alcatel Submarine Networks halted cable-laying operations in the Gulf in March after a ship was stranded near Saudi Arabia, suspending a Meta-backed project to expand internet service across Africa. Repair ships have avoided the region since the US-Iran conflict began in February.

Big Tech companies are now pursuing overland fiber routes from Iraq to Turkey and Europe as alternatives, with IQ Networks coordinating a project along protected oil and gas pipeline routes, Rest of World reported.
sections.whyItMatters: The Strait of Hormuz has become a digital chokepoint as well as an energy transit one. Iran's assertion of control over undersea infrastructure adds a new dimension to Gulf security and could reshape internet routing architecture across the Middle East and into Africa.

sections.heading: US FCC proposes tighter undersea cable rules
sections.body:
The US Federal Communications Commission announced on June 3 plans to toughen oversight of submarine communications cables that handle 99% of international internet traffic, Reuters reported. The proposed rules are designed to help US firms compete more effectively in the global undersea cable market while addressing security concerns about foreign-controlled infrastructure.

The move comes amid heightened scrutiny of undersea infrastructure following China's reported testing of an undersea cable cutter, detailed by Ars Technica in April, and a spate of suspected sabotage incidents in the Red Sea and Baltic Sea.
sections.whyItMatters: Undersea cables are emerging as a contested domain in great power competition. US regulatory tightening could reshape ownership patterns in the $300 million to $1 billion per cable market.

sections.heading: Orbital data centers move toward reality
sections.body:
Muon Space announced on June 3 the Condor-Ultra platform, a Starship-class satellite designed for orbital data centers with 20 kilowatts of baseline power scaling to 100 kilowatts, SpaceNews reported. The platform integrates NVIDIA's Space-1 Vera Rubin Module for AI inferencing and uses Starlink Mini Lasers for inter-satellite data relay. A pathfinder launch is slated for 2028.

The company is opening a new production facility in San Jose, California, with capacity for up to 500 satellites per year. Muon president Greg Smirin said the platform is 'purpose-built for orbital data center and communications missions' and already has customers engaged.

SpaceX, Starcloud, and Cowboy Space have all proposed large-scale orbital data center constellations, signaling a potential shift in computing infrastructure architecture.
sections.whyItMatters: Orbital data centers could reduce latency for certain applications and provide resilience against terrestrial disruptions, but represent a significant escalation in the militarization and commercialization of space.

sections.heading: China's critical minerals strategy outpaces US implementation
sections.body:
China has built a decades-long monopoly in critical mineral refining through coordinated industrial policy, subsidized infrastructure, and vertically integrated processing systems, while US efforts to develop alternative supply chains remain stuck in 'frameworks' and 'memorandums of understanding,' according to a Forbes analysis by Wesley Alexander Hill.

The Lobito Corridor — designed to connect copper and cobalt production in Central Africa to Atlantic export markets — has seen 'financing commitments remain uneven, infrastructure construction advance glacially,' with American investment now expected to taper off. In Kazakhstan, US partnerships on tungsten development show more promise, with Cove Capital and Kazakhstan's National Mining Company announcing a joint venture at what is estimated to be the world's largest unexploited tungsten resource.

The analysis notes that mining projects require 'railways, ports, processing facilities, export financing, power generation, water supplies, and long-term offtake agreements' — adjacent investments the US has struggled to coordinate.
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between US rhetoric and deployed capital on critical minerals is becoming visible to partner governments. China's integrated approach to the entire supply chain ecosystem contrasts with America's 'mining first' strategy that often treats extraction in isolation.

sections.heading: Poland and Taiwan on hybrid warfare frontline
sections.body:
A fake news report that appeared on Poland's national news agency, claiming Prime Minister Donald Tusk was mobilizing 200,000 men, was likely the work of Russia-sponsored hackers, Poland's government said, according to AP via Yahoo News. The incident is part of a broader pattern: Poland has reported cyberattacks on critical infrastructure rising, with Russia's military intelligence trebling resources for such actions this year.

The Diplomat published an analysis positioning Taiwan and Poland as 'parallel frontlines' in hybrid conflict, facing cyber attacks, cognitive warfare, and geopolitical pressure from authoritarian neighbors.
sections.whyItMatters: The Poland incident demonstrates how state-sponsored disinformation can target national infrastructure and public communications, raising questions about resilience in an era of AI-generated deepfakes and rapid information spread.

bottomLine: Three major tech-power developments converged today: Washington tightened AI chip export enforcement by closing the Malaysia subsidiary loophole, Brussels advanced regulatory architecture for digital sovereignty with explicit concern about US legal extraterritoriality, and Iran's threats to undersea cables in the Strait of Hormuz pushed Big Tech toward overland alternatives. Together, these signal that the architecture of digital dependency is being actively contested — through export controls, regulatory frameworks, and infrastructure routing — even as the underlying capabilities remain heavily concentrated.
sources: Asia Business Daily, CNBC, Ars Technica, Reuters, SpaceNews, Forbes, AP via Yahoo News, The Diplomat, EU Observer, Rest of World, TeleGeography, Bloomberg
